planting.note: Plant container or balled-and-burlapped trees in fall or early spring in moist, fertile, well-drained to periodically wet soil near streams or in bottomlands; American sycamore transplants readily when young and establishes quickly in moist sites. Allow ample space — this is one of the largest trees in eastern North America.

directions: Plant in full sun in moist to moderately wet, fertile, deep soil along streams, rivers, or in bottomland sites; American sycamore is one of the largest deciduous trees native to eastern North America, regularly reaching 75–100 feet in height with massive trunks that can exceed 10 feet in diameter in old growth specimens. The tree is instantly recognizable by its spectacular exfoliating bark — the outer bark peels in irregular patches to reveal a patchwork of white, cream, tan, and olive-green inner bark that is particularly striking in winter and visible from great distances. Sycamore is a keystone riparian tree, stabilizing streambanks and providing critical nesting cavities for wood ducks, chimney swifts, and raccoons in hollowed trunks. The small, fuzzy seed balls persist on bare branches through winter and are consumed by goldfinches and purple finches. Despite its preference for moist bottomlands, sycamore adapts well to ordinary landscape soils and is a bold, fast-growing (2–4 feet per year) shade tree for large properties.

planting.note: Plant container or balled-and-burlapped trees in fall or early spring in moist, slightly acidic, well-drained to periodically wet soil in full sun to partial shade; river birch transplants readily and establishes quickly. Multi-stem clump forms are widely available and provide year-round ornamental interest.

directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic, well-drained to periodically flooded soil in full sun to light partial shade; river birch is the most adaptable and heat-tolerant native birch in North America, naturally occurring along streambanks and floodplains from New Hampshire to Florida and west to Kansas. Unlike the European white birch, river birch is highly resistant to the bronze birch borer and bronze birch clearwing, making it a far more reliable long-term landscape tree throughout most of the eastern US. The ornamental exfoliating bark — cinnamon to salmon-pink to creamy-white — peels in papery curls to reveal new inner bark throughout the year, providing outstanding four-season interest. River birch grows quickly (1.5–2 feet per year) and tolerates both wet, poorly drained soils and moderate drought once established, an unusually wide site adaptability. The small winged seeds are relished by chickadees, redpolls, and pine siskins in late winter.

planting.note: Plant container or balled-and-burlapped trees in fall or early spring in moist, slightly acidic, well-drained to moderately wet soil in full sun; red maple establishes readily and grows quickly. Avoid alkaline or compacted soils, which cause chlorosis and stunted growth.

directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic, well-drained to moderately moist soil in full sun for best fall color; red maple is one of the most widely distributed and commonly planted native trees in eastern North America, naturally ranging from Newfoundland to Florida and adaptable to an enormous range of soil moisture conditions from wet bottomlands to dry upland ridges. True to its name, red maple puts on a red display in virtually every season: tiny red flowers cluster on bare branches in late winter (often the earliest tree to bloom), red samaras (winged seeds) ripen in spring, the leaf stalks are red through summer, and fall foliage blazes in brilliant shades of orange-red to crimson. The samaras are consumed by squirrels, grosbeaks, and finches; red maple is also a significant larval host plant for the Cecropia moth and numerous other native insects. With a growth rate of 1.5–2.5 feet per year and wide site adaptability, it is one of the most practical large native shade trees for gardens of all sizes.

planting.note: Plant container-grown trees in fall or early spring in well-drained to moderately moist, slightly acidic to neutral, deep soil in full sun; white oak develops a deep taproot early and transplants best when small. Plant as a long-term investment — this is a multigenerational tree that rewards patience with an irreplaceable landscape presence.

directions: Plant in well-drained to moderately moist, deep, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; white oak is one of the most majestic and ecologically important trees in eastern North America, growing slowly into a massive, wide-spreading giant with deeply furrowed gray bark and a broad, rounded crown that can span 80 feet or more. White oak acorns are widely recognized as the single most nutritious acorn for wildlife — lower in tannins than red oak acorns, they are eagerly consumed by white-tailed deer, wild turkeys, wood ducks, blue jays, and at least 180 other vertebrate species, making white oak the keystone wildlife tree of the eastern deciduous forest. The deeply lobed leaves turn attractive wine-red to russet-brown in fall. Growth is slow but steady — expect 12–15 inches per year — and the tree is exceptionally long-lived (centuries to over 500 years in natural settings). Despite the slow start, there is no native tree that surpasses white oak in landscape longevity, wildlife value, and grandeur.

planting.note: Plant container or balled-and-burlapped trees in fall or early spring in moist, well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; white ash establishes readily and grows at a moderate to fast rate. Consider planting only where emerald ash borer biocontrol programs (parasitoid wasps) are active in your region.

directions: Plant in moist, fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; white ash is a magnificent native canopy tree of eastern North American forests, producing large, compound leaves that turn exceptional shades of yellow, orange, and deep burgundy-purple in fall — among the finest fall color of any native tree. IMPORTANT: White ash is critically threatened across its entire range by the emerald ash borer (EAB), an invasive beetle from Asia that has killed hundreds of millions of ash trees since its detection in Michigan in 2002; all native Fraxinus species in North America are vulnerable. Planting is still worthwhile in areas where USDA-approved biocontrol programs using parasitoid wasps (Tetrastichus planipennisi and others) are actively established, as these programs are showing meaningful suppression in treated areas. Consult your state forestry agency or Cooperative Extension service for current EAB biocontrol status in your region before planting. The historically important wood — used for baseball bats, tool handles, and furniture — and the tree's exceptional ecological value as a host for hundreds of native insects make white ash worth preserving wherever possible.
